name: Venezuelan Arepas
steps: Mix the corn flour with warm water and a pinch of salt into a soft, pliable dough.
Shape into a thick round disc and cook in the oiled skillet over medium heat, 6-7 minutes per side, until golden with dark spots and cooked through.
Split while hot, butter, and stuff with the queso fresco.
steps_detailed: Mix the corn flour with warm water and a pinch of salt, kneading until smooth and non-sticky - add water gradually since the exact amount varies by brand.
Divide into a ball and flatten into a disc about 3/4 inch thick - Venezuelan arepas tend to be thicker than Colombian ones.

Cook in the oiled skillet over medium heat, 6-8 minutes per side, until deeply golden with charred spots, then finish in a 350F (175C) oven for 10 minutes to cook through fully if it's still doughy in the center.
Split most of the way open while hot, butter the inside, and stuff generously with the queso fresco.

name: Venezuelan Perico
steps: Saute the onion in the butter until soft.
Add the diced tomatoes and cook 3-4 minutes until slightly reduced.
Pour in the beaten eggs and scramble gently until just set.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion and bell pepper in the butter over medium heat for 5-6 minutes until soft.
Add the diced tomatoes and cook 4-5 minutes until reduced and slightly jammy.
Beat the eggs and pour into the pan, stirring gently over low heat until just set and creamy.
Stir in the cilantro and serve, traditionally alongside arepas.

name: Venezuelan Cachapa
steps: Blend the corn kernels with the sugar and a pinch of salt into a thick, mostly smooth batter.
Cook spoonfuls in the buttered pan like pancakes, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden.
Fold each warm cachapa around a slice of the queso fresco.
steps_detailed: Blend the corn kernels with the sugar, flour, and a pinch of salt until you have a thick, mostly smooth batter with just a little texture remaining.
Heat a little butter in a nonstick skillet over medium heat and pour in a ladle of batter, spreading slightly into a round about 1/2 inch thick.
Cook 3-4 minutes per side until golden and cooked through, adding a bit more butter between cachapas.
Fold each warm cachapa around a generous slice of queso fresco so it softens slightly, and serve warm.

name: Venezuelan Pabellon Criollo

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Simmer the beef in water until fork-tender, about 40 minutes, then shred it.
Warm the black beans and cook the rice separately.
Fry the plantain slices until golden, then plate everything together.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Simmer the beef with half the onion and garlic in water to cover for 40-45 minutes until completely fork-tender, then shred it finely, reserving some broth.
Saute the remaining onion in the oil until soft, add the diced tomatoes and shredded beef with a splash of the reserved broth, and simmer 8-10 minutes until well combined.
Warm the black beans separately, and cook the rice according to package directions.
Fry the plantain slices in a little oil until golden on both sides.
Arrange the beef, black beans, rice, and fried plantain together on one plate - the four-part 'flag' presentation that gives this dish its name.

name: Venezuelan Sancocho
steps: Simmer the chicken with the onion in water for 20 minutes.
Add the cassava and simmer 15 minutes, then add the corn and simmer 5-8 more minutes.
Season and serve hot with rice.
steps_detailed: Combine the chicken, onion, garlic, and bay leaf with 2.5 cups water in a pot. Simmer 20-22 minutes.
Add the cassava and simmer 15-18 minutes until tender.
Add the corn and simmer 8-10 more minutes.
Remove the bay leaf, stir in the cilantro, and serve hot with rice.

name: Venezuelan Reina Pepiada Arepa Filling
steps: Shred the cooked chicken and mash the avocado together with the sour cream.
Season well with salt.
Make an arepa from the corn flour and stuff it with the chicken-avocado mixture.
steps_detailed: Poach or simmer the chicken breast until cooked through, then shred it finely.
Mash the avocado with the sour cream and lime juice until creamy but still a little chunky.
Fold the shredded chicken and cilantro into the avocado mixture, and season generously with salt.
Make an arepa from the corn flour (see the plain arepa recipe method), split it open, and stuff generously with the chicken-avocado filling - this is the most popular arepa filling in Venezuela.

name: Venezuelan Caraotas Negras
steps: Saute the onion and garlic in the oil until soft.
Add the black beans with a splash of their liquid and simmer 12-15 minutes.
Mash some of the beans to thicken slightly, and season well.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion, garlic, and bell pepper in the oil for 5-6 minutes until soft.
Add the black beans, oregano, and a splash of their liquid, and simmer 15-18 minutes, stirring occasionally.
Mash about a quarter of the beans against the pot to thicken naturally, and stir in the sugar to round out the flavor.
Serve as a side with rice, plantain, and shredded beef.

name: Venezuelan Asado Negro

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Melt the brown sugar in a hot pot until dark and caramelized, then sear the beef in it on all sides.
Add the onion and beef broth, cover, and simmer 1.5-2 hours until completely tender.
Slice and serve with the dark, rich sauce spooned over.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Melt the brown sugar in a heavy pot over medium-high heat, swirling gently, until it deepens to a dark caramel color, watching closely so it doesn't burn.
Sear the beef in the caramelized sugar on all sides, 6-8 minutes total, until well browned.
Add the onion, garlic, beef broth, Worcestershire sauce, and bay leaf.
Cover and simmer over low heat 1.5-2 hours until the beef is completely fork-tender, turning occasionally.
Remove the bay leaf, slice the beef, and serve with the dark braising sauce spooned generously over the top, traditionally with rice and plantain.

name: Venezuelan Pisca Andina
steps: Simmer the potato in water with a pinch of salt until tender, about 12-15 minutes.
Add the milk and warm through, then crack in the egg and poach gently 3-4 minutes.
Top with the cilantro before serving.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion and garlic briefly in a splash of water or oil until just softened.
Add the potato and 1 cup water, and simmer 12-15 minutes until the potato is tender.
Stir in the milk and warm through gently without boiling hard.
Crack the egg directly into the simmering soup and poach gently 3-4 minutes until the white is set.
Top with the cilantro and crumbled queso fresco before serving - a simple, comforting Andean mountain soup.

name: Venezuelan Pollo Guisado
steps: Brown the chicken thigh, then remove and set aside.
Saute the onion and bell pepper, add the tomatoes, and simmer 5 minutes.
Return the chicken and simmer covered 20-25 minutes until tender.
steps_detailed: Season the chicken and brown it over medium-high heat, 4-5 minutes per side, then remove and set aside.
Saute the onion, bell pepper, and garlic in the same pot for 5-6 minutes until softened.
Add the diced tomatoes, chicken broth, and oregano, and simmer 5 minutes.
Return the chicken to the pot, cover, and simmer over low heat 22-25 minutes until tender and cooked through.
Stir in the cilantro and serve with rice.

name: Venezuelan Hallaca-Style Chicken and Cornmeal Bundle
steps: Simmer the chicken with the tomatoes and onion until cooked through, about 20 minutes, then shred.
Mix the corn flour with warm water and achiote for color into a soft, spreadable dough.
Spread the dough on parchment, top with the chicken filling, fold into a sealed packet, and steam 45-50 minutes.
steps_detailed: Simmer the chicken thigh with the diced tomatoes, half the onion, bell pepper, and chicken broth for 20-22 minutes until cooked through, then shred and stir the mixture together with the olives.
Whisk the achiote paste with warm water and the remaining onion, then mix into the corn flour with the vegetable oil to form a soft, spreadable, golden-orange dough.
Spread a portion of the dough onto a square of parchment paper, top with the chicken filling, and fold the parchment over to fully enclose it into a sealed packet, tying if needed.
Steam the packets over simmering water for 45-55 minutes until the masa is firm.
Traditionally made with banana leaves and a much more elaborate filling for Christmas - this is a simplified year-round version of the same idea.

name: Venezuelan Tequenos
steps: Make a simple dough from the flour, butter, a pinch of salt, and enough water to form a smooth ball. Rest 15 minutes.
Roll thin and cut into strips, then wrap each strip around a stick of the cheese, sealing the ends.
Fry in the oil until golden on all sides, about 3-4 minutes total.
steps_detailed: Make a smooth dough from the flour, butter, egg, a pinch of salt, and just enough water to bring it together. Rest 15-20 minutes.
Roll the dough thin and cut into long strips about 3/4 inch wide.
Wrap each strip around a stick of queso fresco in a spiral, pinching the ends to seal completely so the cheese can't leak out while frying.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the tequeños, turning occasionally, 3-5 minutes until golden brown on all sides and the cheese inside is warm and soft.
Drain briefly and serve hot - these are a Venezuelan party staple.

name: Venezuelan Empanadas
steps: Mix the corn flour with warm water and a pinch of salt into a soft dough.
Brown the beef with the onion, season well, and let cool.
Shape the dough into discs, fill with the beef, fold and seal, then fry until golden and crisp.
steps_detailed: Mix the corn flour with warm water and a pinch of salt, kneading until a smooth, pliable dough forms.
Saute the onion and garlic, then add the ground beef and cumin, browning fully, 6-8 minutes. Let the filling cool.
Flatten small balls of dough into thin discs, place a spoonful of filling in the center, and fold into a half-moon, pressing the edges to seal well.
Heat the oil in a skillet over medium heat and fry the empanadas 2-3 minutes per side until deep golden and crisp.
Drain on a paper towel and serve hot.

name: Venezuelan Cachapa con Queso
steps: Blend the corn with the sugar and a pinch of salt into a thick batter.
Cook small rounds in the buttered pan, 3 minutes per side, until golden.
Fold around the cheese while warm.
steps_detailed: Blend the corn kernels with the sugar and a pinch of salt into a thick, mostly smooth batter.
Cook small rounds (about 4 inches) in the buttered pan over medium heat, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden.
Fold each one around a slice of queso fresco while still warm so the cheese softens, and serve as a snack-sized portion.

name: Venezuelan Quesillo
steps: Melt the sugar in a small baking dish over low heat until it forms a caramel coating the bottom.
Whisk the milk, condensed milk, and eggs together, then pour over the caramel.
Bake in a water bath at 325F for 35-40 minutes until just set, then chill and unmold.
steps_detailed: Melt the sugar in a small baking dish over low heat (or briefly in the oven) until it becomes a thin, dark caramel coating the bottom.
Whisk together the milk, condensed milk, eggs, and vanilla until smooth and well combined.
Pour the custard mixture over the caramel layer.
Set the dish into a larger pan with hot water halfway up the sides, and bake at 325F (165C) for 35-45 minutes until just set with a gentle wobble.
Chill thoroughly, at least 2 hours, then run a knife around the edge and invert onto a plate to unmold.

name: Venezuelan Bienmesabe
steps: Whisk the egg with a little sugar and the flour into a thin batter, then bake or steam into a thin sponge layer.
Simmer the coconut milk with the remaining sugar for 8-10 minutes into a thick, sweet coconut cream.
Layer the sponge with the coconut cream and chill before serving.
steps_detailed: Beat the eggs with half the sugar until pale, then fold in the flour and baking powder to make a simple sponge batter.
Bake at 350F (175C) in a small dish for 12-15 minutes until springy and lightly golden, then let cool and crumble or slice into layers.
Simmer the coconut milk with the remaining sugar over medium-low heat for 10-12 minutes until thickened into a rich, sweet coconut cream.
Layer the sponge with the coconut cream in a dish or individual glasses, finishing with a layer of cream on top.
Sprinkle with the toasted shredded coconut and chill at least 30 minutes before serving.

name: Venezuelan Dulce de Lechosa
steps: Simmer the papaya nectar with the brown sugar and cinnamon for 15-20 minutes until reduced to a thick syrup.
Traditionally made with green papaya chunks candied slowly - this captures the same sweet, spiced flavor.
Cool and serve in small spoonfuls.
steps_detailed: Combine the papaya nectar, brown sugar, and cinnamon in a small saucepan.
Simmer over medium-low heat, stirring occasionally, for 20-25 minutes until reduced to a thick, glossy syrup.
Let cool to room temperature - it thickens further as it sits.
Serve in small spoonfuls with a bit of queso fresco alongside, the traditional sweet-and-salty pairing.

name: Venezuelan Majarete
steps: Whisk the corn flour into the coconut milk with the sugar until smooth.
Simmer over low heat, stirring constantly, for 8-10 minutes until thickened into a soft pudding.
Pour into a dish, chill, and dust with cinnamon before serving.
steps_detailed: Whisk the corn flour into the whole milk first to form a smooth slurry with no lumps.
Warm the coconut milk with the sugar in a saucepan until steaming.
Whisk in the corn flour slurry and simmer over low heat, stirring constantly, for 10-12 minutes until thickened into a soft, custardy pudding.
Pour into small dishes or one larger dish and chill at least an hour until set.
Dust generously with cinnamon just before serving.

name: Venezuelan Guasacaca
steps: Mash the avocado with the cilantro, onion, and lime juice until mostly smooth but still a little chunky.
Season with salt.
Serve alongside arepas, grilled meat, or empanadas.
steps_detailed: Mash the avocado with the cilantro, onion, garlic, and bell pepper, keeping some texture rather than making it fully smooth.
Stir in the lime juice and oil, and season generously with salt.
Let sit 10 minutes before serving so the flavors meld - this is Venezuela's everyday green sauce, served with almost everything.

name: Venezuelan Mojo de Ajo
steps: Combine the minced garlic, oil, and lime juice in a small bowl.
Season with salt and let sit 10 minutes before serving.
Spoon over grilled meat or yuca.
steps_detailed: Combine the minced garlic, oil, lime juice, cilantro, and black pepper in a small bowl.
Season well with salt and let sit at room temperature 15-20 minutes so the garlic mellows slightly.
Spoon generously over grilled meats, boiled yuca, or fried plantain.
